title: How States Can Improve School Choice
title_cn: 各州如何改进学校选择

paragraphs.en: The best case for school choice is that parents are free to take it or leave it—and they’re taking it in droves. Some 1.5 million students are using private choice programs in the 2025-26 school year, up from 1.2 million last year, according to the nonprofit EdChoice. But not all parents who want private options can get them, and state lawmakers can still do more to help.
paragraphs.cn: 学校选择的最佳理由是家长可以自由选择接受或放弃——而他们正在蜂拥而至地选择它。根据非营利组织EdChoice的数据，2025-26学年约有150万学生正在使用私人选择项目，高于去年的120万。但并非所有想要私人选择的家长都能得到，州立法者仍能做更多来提供帮助。

paragraphs.en: The great news is that some 19 states now have universal choice programs, meaning any student is eligible. Iowa this school year opened K-12 education savings accounts (ESAs) to all, and 41,044 students are using them, about 13,000 more than last year. So did Arkansas, which awarded more than 46,000 ESAs this fall, up from about 14,000 last year.
paragraphs.cn: 好消息是，目前约有19个州拥有普惠性选择项目，这意味着任何学生都有资格。爱荷华州本学年向所有学生开放了K-12教育储蓄账户（ESAs），有41,044名学生正在使用，比去年增加了约13,000人。阿肯色州也如此，今年秋季发放了超过46,000个ESA，高于去年的约14,000个。

paragraphs.en: New Hampshire’s ESA enrollment jumped to 10,000 students this year from 5,800 with the removal of an income eligibility cap. In Arizona, where ESAs have been available to all students since 2022, more than 100,000 are using them, up from 85,000 last spring.
paragraphs.cn: 新罕布什尔州的ESA入学人数今年从5,800人跃升至10,000人，原因是取消了收入资格上限。在亚利桑那州，ESA自2022年以来一直对所有学生开放，目前有超过100,000人正在使用，高于去年春季的85,000人。

paragraphs.en: The problem is that not all “universal” programs can meet demand. More than 40,000 students applied for Tennessee’s ESA program, but it’s capped by law at 20,000 this school year. Louisiana lawmakers funded 6,000 ESAs this school year, and thousands of eligible families were denied. Texas’ new ESAs, effective next school year, are open to the state’s six million students, but $1 billion in initial funding means only as many as 90,000 children will be able to get them.
paragraphs.cn: 问题在于并非所有“普惠性”项目都能满足需求。超过40,000名学生申请了田纳西州的ESA项目，但本学年法律规定上限为20,000人。路易斯安那州立法者本学年资助了6,000个ESA，但数千个符合条件的家庭被拒绝。德克萨斯州的新ESA将于下学年生效，对该州六百万学生开放，但10亿美元的初始资金意味着只有多达90,000名儿童能够获得。

paragraphs.en: Scholarship amounts also limit families’ choices. Private school tuition costs on average nearly $15,000 a year, according to the Private School Review. The average ESA is about half of that. In a 2025 survey of Florida parents who didn’t use the ESAs they were awarded, nearly 20% said it wasn’t enough for tuition.
paragraphs.cn: 奖学金金额也限制了家庭的选择。根据Private School Review的数据，私立学校的学费平均每年近15,000美元。平均每个ESA大约是这个数字的一半。在2025年对佛罗里达州未使用的ESA的家长进行的一项调查中，近20%的家长表示这不足以支付学费。

paragraphs.en: To create truly universal programs, states can remove enrollment caps and fund ESAs outside of annual appropriations, following the example of Arizona and Arkansas. They can boost scholarship amounts, which are often a fraction of the money per pupil that traditional K-12 districts get. States can also make sure parents are permitted to use the dollars for a variety of expenses, including in home schooling and microschools. A good education market includes many models.
paragraphs.cn: 为了创建真正普惠性的项目，各州可以取消入学上限，并在年度拨款之外为ESA提供资金，效仿亚利桑那州和阿肯色州的例子。他们可以提高奖学金金额，这些金额通常只是传统K-12学区每名学生获得资金的一小部分。各州还可以确保家长被允许将这些资金用于各种开支，包括家庭教育和微型学校。一个良好的教育市场包含多种模式。

paragraphs.en: Teachers unions say that ESAs drain money from public schools while busting state budgets. Yet Arizona’s spending on private school choice is only about 9% of its K-12 spending, according to EdChoice. Florida’s figure is 11%, and in most other states it’s less than 5%.
paragraphs.cn: 教师工会表示，ESA从公立学校抽走资金，同时使州预算破产。然而，根据EdChoice的数据，亚利桑那州在私立学校选择上的支出仅占其K-12支出的约9%。佛罗里达州的数字是11%，而在大多数其他州则低于5%。

paragraphs.en: The real fiscal problem is that lawmakers often lack the political will to let the money follow the student, even if that’s out of a failing public school that really ought to be closed. Instead, states grease the passage of choice programs with extra money for public schools, which only weakens their incentive to improve.
paragraphs.cn: 真正的财政问题是，立法者往往缺乏政治意愿让资金跟着学生走，即使那笔钱来自一所本应关闭的失败公立学校。相反，各州通过向公立学校提供额外资金来促成选择项目的通过，这只会削弱它们改进的动力。

paragraphs.en: Still, the momentum for school choice is likely to continue. The Mississippi House passed a bill recently to establish ESAs open to any student, though it faces opposition in the Senate. Tennessee Gov. Bill Lee has said he wants to increase ESA funding to help clear the wait list. Governors in more than half the states have said they’ll opt into the federal tax-credit scholarships passed by Congress last year, which won’t add to state education costs.
paragraphs.cn: 尽管如此，学校选择的势头很可能会继续。密西西比州众议院最近通过了一项法案，旨在设立对任何学生开放的ESA，尽管它在参议院面临反对。田纳西州州长比尔·李表示，他希望增加ESA资金以帮助清理等候名单。超过一半州的州长表示，他们将选择加入国会去年通过的联邦税收抵免奖学金，这不会增加州的教育成本。

paragraphs.en: “The vouchers are needed,” one Tennessee mother, who used an ESA to send her daughter to private school, told WKRN. “I make less than $50,000 a year. How am I going to afford a school that costs $22,000?” The job of lawmakers in Tennessee, and across the country, is to answer the question for that mother and many others.
paragraphs.cn: “这些代金券是必需的，”一位田纳西州的母亲告诉WKRN，她使用ESA将女儿送进了私立学校。“我每年收入不到50,000美元。我怎么能负担得起一所学费22,000美元的学校呢？”田纳西州乃至全国的立法者的职责，就是为这位母亲和许多其他人回答这个问题。

paragraphs.en: The Editorial Board speaks for free markets and free people, the principles, if you will, marked in the watershed year of 1776 by Thomas Jefferson's Declaration of Independence and Adam Smith's “Wealth of Nations.” So over the past century and into the next, the Journal stands for free trade and sound money; against confiscatory taxation and the ukases of kings and other collectivists; and for individual autonomy against dictators, bullies and even the tempers of momentary majorities.
paragraphs.cn: 编辑委员会代表自由市场和自由人民，这些原则，如果你愿意，是托马斯·杰斐逊的《独立宣言》和亚当·斯密的《国富论》在1776年这个分水岭之年所标志的。因此，在过去一个世纪和未来，本报支持自由贸易和稳健货币；反对没收性税收以及国王和其他集体主义者的敕令；并支持个人自治，反对独裁者、欺凌者甚至短暂多数人的脾气。

vocabulary.meaning_en: in large numbers; in a crowd.
vocabulary.example: The best case for school choice is that parents are free to take it or leave it—and they’re taking it in droves.
vocabulary.example_cn: 学校选择的最佳理由是家长可以自由选择接受或放弃——而他们正在蜂拥而至地选择它。

vocabulary.meaning_en: (of an organization) not making or conducted primarily to make a profit.
vocabulary.example: Some 1.5 million students are using private choice programs in the 2025-26 school year, up from 1.2 million last year, according to the nonprofit EdChoice.
vocabulary.example_cn: 根据非营利组织EdChoice的数据，2025-26学年约有150万学生正在使用私人选择项目，高于去年的120万。

vocabulary.meaning_en: having the right to do or obtain something; satisfying the appropriate conditions.
vocabulary.example: The great news is that some 19 states now have universal choice programs, meaning any student is eligible.
vocabulary.example_cn: 好消息是，目前约有19个州拥有普惠性选择项目，这意味着任何学生都有资格。

vocabulary.meaning_en: the action of enrolling or being enrolled.
vocabulary.example: New Hampshire’s ESA enrollment jumped to 10,000 students this year from 5,800 with the removal of an income eligibility cap.
vocabulary.example_cn: 新罕布什尔州的ESA入学人数今年从5,800人跃升至10,000人，原因是取消了收入资格上限。

vocabulary.meaning_cn: 设上限的；限制在一定数量的。
vocabulary.example: More than 40,000 students applied for Tennessee’s ESA program, but it’s capped by law at 20,000 this school year.
vocabulary.example_cn: 超过40,000名学生申请了田纳西州的ESA项目，但本学年法律规定上限为20,000人。

vocabulary.meaning_en: a sum of money charged for teaching or instruction by a school, college, or university.
vocabulary.example: Private school tuition costs on average nearly $15,000 a year, according to the Private School Review.
vocabulary.example_cn: 根据Private School Review的数据，私立学校的学费平均每年近15,000美元。

vocabulary.meaning_en: money set aside for a specific purpose, especially by a government.
vocabulary.example: To create truly universal programs, states can remove enrollment caps and fund ESAs outside of annual appropriations, following the example of Arizona and Arkansas.
vocabulary.example_cn: 为了创建真正普惠性的项目，各州可以取消入学上限，并在年度拨款之外为ESA提供资金，效仿亚利桑那州和阿肯色州的例子。

vocabulary.meaning_en: relating to government revenue, especially taxes.
vocabulary.example: The real fiscal problem is that lawmakers often lack the political will to let the money follow the student, even if that’s out of a failing public school that really ought to be closed.
vocabulary.example_cn: 真正的财政问题是，立法者往往缺乏政治意愿让资金跟着学生走，即使那笔钱来自一所本应关闭的失败公立学校。

vocabulary.meaning_en: the impetus gained by a moving object; the force or speed of movement.
vocabulary.example: Still, the momentum for school choice is likely to continue.
vocabulary.example_cn: 尽管如此，学校选择的势头很可能会继续。

vocabulary.meaning_en: have enough money to pay for.
vocabulary.example: “I make less than $50,000 a year. How am I going to afford a school that costs $22,000?”
vocabulary.example_cn: “我每年收入不到50,000美元。我怎么能负担得起一所学费22,000美元的学校呢？”
